purpose: Separates claimable product readiness from local proof/closeout readiness, and records the language-fit contract for JS control plane versus Python/Rust/native substrates.

over_100_hill_climb.base_score_meaning: 100 means every readiness requirement is either claimably shipped/implemented or has an executable local closeout/workorder contract.
over_100_hill_climb.surplus_meaning: Points above 100 count extra local files, evidence requirements, and closeout detail beyond the W599 minimum. They do not convert external gates into shipped claims.

language_fit.interpretation: JavaScript is acceptable for API, CLI, web, orchestration, and generated proof ledgers; Python/Rust/native substrates must exist for ML workers, runtime verification, WASM, mobile clients, and hot execution paths.

escape_hatches.reason: frontier quantization methods belong in Python/native ML tooling
FP4 calibration should not be locked into the JS control plane
preference optimization needs Python trainer/library access
GKD/on-policy distillation needs Python trainer/library access
large-pair dedup belongs in worker/data substrate
DSIR and curation statistics belong in data-worker substrate
near-duplicate indexing belongs outside request-path JS
runtime verification needs a memory-safe native substrate
artifact verification should have a standalone native path
browser/edge runtime needs a WASM-capable native core
ZIP parsing and bounds checks are proof-critical runtime code
Python SDK needs its own ONNX runtime escape hatch
JS should orchestrate native ONNX execution rather than reimplement kernels
JS should dispatch native runners for hot execution paths
iOS clients need a native SDK surface
Android clients need a native SDK surface
React Native needs native iOS bridge code
React Native needs native Android bridge code

warnings.note: Local proof coverage is 100%, but these rows still require partner/package/public benchmark/certification evidence before they can become claimable shipped readiness.
